ascii-text-drawer.title: ASCII Text Drawer / Art Generator
ascii-text-drawer.description: Free online ASCII art text generator. Convert any text into large block letters, shadow art, or simple ASCII banners — no external libraries required.

whatIs.heading: What Is an ASCII Text Drawer?
whatIs.content: An ASCII Text Drawer converts plain text into decorative ASCII art using characters like #, █, and ░. Each letter is rendered as a multi-line glyph on a fixed grid, creating large eye-catching banners from ordinary text. This tool includes three built-in styles — Block, Shadow, and Simple — with no external dependencies.

howToUse.heading: How to Use the ASCII Text Drawer
steps: Type or paste the text you want to convert into the input field.
Select a rendering style: Block (5-line tall # letters), Shadow (block letters with shadow effect), or Simple (compact 3-line letters).
View the generated ASCII art in the output area below.
Click the Copy button to copy the output to your clipboard.

items.title: README Banners
items.description: Add stylish ASCII art headers to README files and documentation to make project names and section titles stand out.

items.title: Terminal Welcome Messages

items.title: Code Comments

items.title: Email & Chat Signatures

faq.question: What characters are supported?
faq.answer: The built-in font maps support uppercase A–Z, digits 0–9, space, and a few punctuation marks (!, ., -, _). Any other characters are replaced with a fallback glyph. Input is automatically converted to uppercase.

faq.question: What is the difference between Block and Shadow styles?
faq.answer: Block style renders each character as a 5-line tall grid of # symbols. Shadow style uses the same grid but renders with █ (full block) characters and adds a ░ (light shade) shadow offset to create a 3D depth effect.

faq.question: Does this tool use figlet.js or any external library?
faq.answer: No. This tool uses a fully self-contained font map defined directly in the component. There are no external dependencies or font files required.

faq.question: Can I use the output in a monospaced context only?
faq.answer: Yes. ASCII art relies on every character occupying the same width. It will look correct in terminals, code editors, pre tags, Markdown code blocks, and any environment that uses a monospaced (fixed-width) font.

base64-file-converter.title: Base64 File Converter — Free Online
base64-file-converter.description: Convert files to Base64 and Base64 back to files with this free online tool. Supports images, PDFs, and any binary file. No sign-up required.

whatIs.heading: What is Base64 File Encoding?
whatIs.content: Base64 file encoding converts any binary file — images, PDFs, audio, executables — into a plain-text ASCII string using the Base64 alphabet (A-Z, a-z, 0-9, +, /). This is especially useful when you need to embed binary content in text-only formats such as JSON payloads, HTML data URIs, CSS backgrounds, or email attachments. A data URI combines the MIME type with the Base64 string (e.g. data:image/png;base64,iVBOR...) so browsers can render the file directly from the string without an extra HTTP request.

howToUse.heading: How to Use This Free Online Base64 File Converter
steps: Choose a mode: 'File → Base64' to encode, or 'Base64 → File' to decode.
To encode: drag and drop a file or click to upload. The Base64 output appears instantly.
Toggle 'Include data URI prefix' on or off depending on whether you need the full data URI or raw Base64.
Click the Copy button to copy the result to your clipboard.
To decode: paste a Base64 string (with or without the data URI prefix) and click Download File.

useCases.heading: Common Use Cases for Base64 File Conversion

items.title: Embedding images in HTML or CSS
items.description: Convert small icons and logos to data URIs so they load inline without additional HTTP requests, improving page performance.

items.title: Sending files through JSON APIs
items.description: Many REST APIs accept file uploads as Base64-encoded strings inside JSON bodies, avoiding multipart form handling.

items.title: Storing files in text-based databases
items.description: Some NoSQL or configuration stores only accept text values. Base64 lets you persist binary data safely.

items.title: Email attachment encoding
items.description: MIME email standards encode binary attachments as Base64 so they can travel through text-only email transport layers.

faq.question: What file types can I convert to Base64?
faq.answer: Any file type works — images (PNG, JPG, GIF, SVG, WebP), PDFs, audio files, videos, ZIP archives, and more. Base64 encoding is format-agnostic; it simply converts raw bytes to text.

faq.question: Why is the Base64 output larger than the original file?
faq.answer: Base64 encoding increases size by approximately 33% because it maps every 3 bytes to 4 ASCII characters. For large files, consider using direct binary transfer instead.

faq.question: Is my file uploaded to a server?
faq.answer: No. All conversion happens entirely in your browser using the JavaScript FileReader API. Your files never leave your device.

faq.question: What is a data URI prefix?
faq.answer: A data URI (e.g. data:image/png;base64,...) embeds the MIME type alongside the Base64 string so browsers know how to interpret the data. It's required for inline images in HTML/CSS but not needed for API payloads.

base64-string-converter.title: Base64 String Encoder & Decoder
base64-string-converter.description: Encode and decode Base64 strings instantly with this free online converter. Supports standard and URL-safe Base64. No sign-up required, runs entirely in your browser.

whatIs.heading: What is Base64 Encoding?
whatIs.content: Base64 is a binary-to-text encoding scheme that converts binary data into a printable ASCII string format. It represents binary data using 64 characters (A-Z, a-z, 0-9, +, /) plus '=' for padding. Base64 encoding is widely used in web development to embed binary content — such as images, fonts, or file attachments — inside text-based formats like JSON, HTML, CSS, and email (MIME). The encoding follows the specification defined in RFC 4648. Because each group of 3 bytes becomes 4 Base64 characters, encoded output is roughly 33% larger than the original data.

howToUse.heading: How to Use This Free Online Base64 Encoder & Decoder
steps: Paste or type the text you want to encode or decode into the input field.
Select the mode: Encode (text → Base64) or Decode (Base64 → text).
The result appears instantly in the output field — no button click needed.
Click the Copy button to copy the result to your clipboard.

useCases.heading: Common Use Cases for Base64 Encoding

items.title: Embedding images in HTML or CSS
items.description: Convert small images to Base64 data URIs to reduce HTTP requests and inline them directly in your markup or stylesheets.

items.title: Encoding data in JSON APIs
items.description: Safely transmit binary payloads (like file content or cryptographic signatures) inside JSON strings without escaping issues.

items.title: Email attachments (MIME)

items.title: HTTP Basic Authentication headers
items.description: The Authorization header encodes the username:password pair in Base64 before sending it to the server.

items.title: Storing binary data in text-only databases or configs
items.description: When a storage system only accepts text, Base64 lets you safely persist binary blobs without corruption.

faq.question: Is Base64 encoding the same as encryption?
faq.answer: No. Base64 is an encoding scheme, not encryption. It does not provide any security — anyone can decode a Base64 string back to the original data. For secure data protection, use AES or another encryption algorithm.

faq.question: Why does Base64 increase the data size by about 33%?
faq.answer: Base64 encodes every 3 bytes of input into 4 ASCII characters. This 4/3 ratio means the output is approximately 33% larger than the input. Padding characters ('=') may add a few extra bytes.

faq.question: What is URL-safe Base64?
faq.answer: Standard Base64 uses '+' and '/' characters, which have special meaning in URLs. URL-safe Base64 (also called Base64url, defined in RFC 4648 §5) replaces '+' with '-' and '/' with '_', and typically omits padding. This makes the encoded string safe to use in URLs and filenames.

faq.question: Is this tool free and private?
faq.answer: Yes. This tool is completely free, requires no sign-up, and runs entirely in your browser using JavaScript. No data is sent to any server — your input stays on your device.

basic-auth-generator-decoder.title: Basic Auth Generator & Decoder - HTTP Authorization Header
basic-auth-generator-decoder.description: Free online Basic Auth header generator and decoder. Create HTTP Basic Authorization headers from credentials or decode existing headers instantly.

whatIs.heading: What Is a Basic Auth Generator & Decoder?
whatIs.content: A Basic Auth Generator & Decoder is a tool for working with HTTP Basic Authentication headers. HTTP Basic Auth is a simple authentication scheme built into the HTTP protocol where the client sends a username and password encoded in Base64 as part of the Authorization header. The format is 'Basic base64(username:password)'. This tool lets you generate a properly formatted header from credentials, or decode an existing header back into its username and password components. It's commonly used when working with REST APIs, web servers, and development environments that require Basic Authentication.

howToUse.heading: How to Use the Basic Auth Tool
steps: Select the 'Generate' tab to create an Authorization header, or 'Decode' to extract credentials from one.
To generate: enter a username and password. The tool instantly creates the 'Basic encoded' header string.
Copy the generated header using the copy button to use in API requests, curl commands, or configuration files.
To decode: paste a 'Basic ...' Authorization header value. The tool decodes the Base64 and displays the username and password.
The tool automatically handles the 'Basic ' prefix in both modes — you can paste with or without it.

items.title: API Testing
items.description: Generate Basic Auth headers for use in tools like curl, Postman, or fetch requests when testing APIs that require Basic Authentication.

items.title: Configuration Setup
items.description: Create encoded authorization strings for web server configuration files, proxy settings, or CI/CD pipeline variables.

items.title: Debugging Auth Issues
items.description: Decode Authorization headers from HTTP logs or network captures to verify that the correct credentials are being sent.

items.title: Documentation
items.description: Generate example Authorization headers for API documentation, showing developers the exact format they need to use.

faq.question: Is HTTP Basic Auth secure?
faq.answer: Basic Auth encodes credentials in Base64, which is trivially reversible — it is not encryption. It should only be used over HTTPS connections. For production applications, consider more secure alternatives like OAuth2 Bearer tokens or API keys.

faq.question: Can I use special characters in the password?
faq.answer: Yes. The username and password are joined with a colon and Base64-encoded together. Special characters, spaces, and unicode characters are all supported, though some older servers may have issues with non-ASCII characters.

faq.question: Why does decoding show the wrong password?
faq.answer: If the password contains a colon character, the decoder splits on the first colon only, so the password will include any subsequent colons. This matches the HTTP Basic Auth specification (RFC 7617) where only the first colon is treated as the separator.

faq.question: How do I use the generated header in a curl command?
faq.answer: Use the -H flag: curl -H 'Authorization: Basic dXNlcjpwYXNz' https://api.example.com. Alternatively, curl has built-in Basic Auth support with the -u flag: curl -u username:password https://api.example.com.

bcrypt-hash-generator.title: Bcrypt Hash Generator & Verifier
bcrypt-hash-generator.description: Generate and verify bcrypt password hashes with adjustable cost factor using this free online tool. No sign-up required, runs entirely in your browser.

whatIs.heading: What Is Bcrypt Hashing?
whatIs.content: Bcrypt is a password-hashing function designed by Niels Provos and David Mazières based on the Blowfish cipher. Unlike fast hash algorithms like SHA-256, bcrypt is intentionally slow and includes a configurable cost factor (work factor) that controls how computationally expensive it is to produce a hash. This makes brute-force attacks significantly harder. Bcrypt also automatically generates and embeds a random salt in each hash, so identical passwords produce different hash values. The output format ($2a$, $2b$, or $2y$ prefix) encodes the algorithm version, cost factor, salt, and hash in a single 60-character string.

howToUse.heading: How to Use This Bcrypt Hash Tool
steps: To generate a hash: enter a password, adjust the cost factor (10 is a good default), and click Generate.
The bcrypt hash appears instantly — copy it for storage in your database.
To verify: switch to the Verify tab, enter the original password and the stored hash.
Click Verify to check if the password matches the hash.

items.title: Secure password storage
items.description: Hash user passwords with bcrypt before storing them in a database. The built-in salt and adjustable cost factor protect against rainbow table and brute-force attacks.

items.title: Password migration testing
items.description: When migrating authentication systems, quickly verify that existing bcrypt hashes still match their original passwords.

items.title: Cost factor benchmarking
items.description: Test different cost factors to find the right balance between security and login performance for your server hardware.

items.title: Development and debugging
items.description: Generate sample bcrypt hashes for use in test fixtures, seed data, or API development without needing a running backend.

faq.question: What cost factor should I use?
faq.answer: A cost factor of 10 is a widely used default. Each increment doubles the computation time. For high-security applications, use 12 or higher, but ensure it does not make your login flow too slow — aim for under 250ms per hash on your server.

faq.question: Why do identical passwords produce different bcrypt hashes?
faq.answer: Bcrypt generates a unique random salt for every hash operation. The salt is embedded in the output string, so even the same password hashed twice will produce different results. Verification still works because bcrypt extracts the salt from the stored hash.

faq.question: Is bcrypt better than SHA-256 for passwords?
faq.answer: Yes. SHA-256 is designed to be fast, which makes it vulnerable to brute-force attacks on passwords. Bcrypt is intentionally slow and includes a tunable cost factor, making it far more resistant to password cracking.

faq.question: Is this tool safe to use for real passwords?
faq.answer: The hashing runs entirely in your browser — no data is sent to any server. However, for production password storage, always hash passwords on the server side to prevent exposure of plaintext passwords in transit.

whatIs.heading: What Is a BIP39 Mnemonic?
whatIs.content: BIP39 (Bitcoin Improvement Proposal 39) defines a standard for generating mnemonic phrases from random entropy. These human-readable word sequences (typically 12 or 24 words) encode the master seed for hierarchical deterministic (HD) wallets used in Bitcoin, Ethereum, and many other cryptocurrencies. The mnemonic includes a checksum to detect transcription errors.

howToUse.heading: How to Use This BIP39 Generator
steps: Select the number of words (12, 15, 18, 21, or 24).
Click Generate Mnemonic to create a cryptographically secure seed phrase.
Copy the phrase and store it securely offline. Never share your real wallet seed phrase online.
Use the Validate section to check if an existing mnemonic has a valid BIP39 checksum.

faq.question: Should I use this for my real cryptocurrency wallet?
faq.answer: This tool is designed for testing and educational purposes. For real wallets, use a hardware wallet or trusted wallet software that generates mnemonics offline on a trusted device.

faq.question: How is the entropy generated?
faq.answer: The tool uses the Web Crypto API crypto.getRandomValues() for cryptographically secure random number generation, the same source used by browsers for TLS and other security operations.

faq.question: What is the checksum in BIP39?
faq.answer: BIP39 appends a SHA-256 checksum to the entropy bits before splitting into 11-bit word indices. For a 12-word mnemonic (128 bits entropy), 4 checksum bits are appended. This catches transcription errors.

faq.question: What does chmod 755 mean?
faq.answer: chmod 755 sets permissions to rwxr-xr-x, meaning the owner can read, write, and execute the file, while group members and other users can only read and execute it. This is commonly used for executable scripts and web directories.

faq.question: What is the difference between octal and symbolic notation?
faq.answer: Octal notation uses three digits (e.g., 644) where each digit represents permissions for owner, group, and others. Symbolic notation uses letters r (read), w (write), and x (execute) with dashes for denied permissions (e.g., rw-r--r--). Both represent the same permissions.

faq.question: What permission should I use for SSH keys?
faq.answer: SSH private keys should be set to 600 (rw-------), meaning only the owner can read and write the file. The SSH directory itself should be 700 (rwx------). SSH will refuse to use keys with overly permissive settings.

faq.question: Why should I avoid chmod 777?
faq.answer: chmod 777 grants full read, write, and execute permissions to everyone, creating a significant security risk. Any user on the system can modify or execute the file, which could lead to unauthorized access or code execution.

CIDR replaced the old classful addressing system and is the standard way to define IP address ranges in modern networking.

howToUse.heading: How to Use the CIDR Calculator

faq.question: What does the /24 in CIDR notation mean?
faq.answer: The number after the slash is the prefix length — the number of bits used for the network portion. /24 means the first 24 bits are the network part, leaving 8 bits for host addresses, giving 254 usable hosts (2^8 - 2).

faq.question: Why are there 2 fewer usable hosts than the total addresses?
faq.answer: In any subnet, the first address is the network address and the last is the broadcast address. Neither can be assigned to a host, so usable hosts = 2^(32-prefix) - 2. The exceptions are /31 (point-to-point links) and /32 (single host).

faq.question: Does this support IPv6?
faq.answer: Currently this tool supports IPv4 CIDR notation only. IPv6 support may be added in the future.

It removes all original styling and enforces a consistent code style, making your codebase easier to read and maintain.

color-contrast-checker.title: Accessibility Color Contrast Checker
color-contrast-checker.description: Free online WCAG color contrast checker. Calculate contrast ratios, verify AA/AAA compliance, preview text readability, and simulate color blindness for accessible design.

whatIs.heading: What Is a Color Contrast Checker?
whatIs.content: A color contrast checker measures the luminance difference between a foreground (text) color and a background color, producing a contrast ratio as defined by the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1. This ratio determines whether text is readable for users with low vision or color deficiencies. WCAG defines two conformance levels — AA (minimum) and AAA (enhanced) — each with different thresholds for normal-sized text and large text. This tool calculates the ratio instantly and also simulates how your color pair appears under common forms of color blindness.

howToUse.heading: How to Use the Color Contrast Checker
steps: Enter your foreground (text) color as a HEX value (e.g., #1e293b) or use the color picker.
Enter your background color as a HEX value (e.g., #ffffff) or use the color picker.
View the live preview showing your text at different sizes against the background.
Check the contrast ratio and WCAG AA/AAA pass or fail results displayed below.
Use the Swap Colors button to quickly reverse foreground and background.
Scroll down to see color blindness simulations for protanopia, deuteranopia, and tritanopia.

items.title: Web Accessibility Compliance
items.description: Verify that your website's text and background color combinations meet WCAG 2.1 AA or AAA requirements before deployment.

items.title: Design System Validation
items.description: Test all color token pairings in your design system to ensure every combination is accessible and compliant.

items.title: Color Blindness Testing
items.description: Preview how users with protanopia, deuteranopia, or tritanopia perceive your color choices and adjust accordingly.

items.title: Client & Stakeholder Reports

faq.question: What contrast ratio is required for WCAG AA compliance?
faq.answer: WCAG AA requires a minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1 for normal-sized text and 3:1 for large text (18px+ or 14px+ bold). These thresholds ensure readability for most users with moderate vision impairments.

faq.question: What is the difference between WCAG AA and AAA?
faq.answer: WCAG AAA is a stricter standard requiring 7:1 for normal text and 4.5:1 for large text. While AA is considered the minimum acceptable level, AAA provides enhanced readability and is recommended for body text on high-traffic or government websites.

faq.question: How does the color blindness simulation work?
faq.answer: The tool applies scientifically-derived color transformation matrices that approximate how people with protanopia (red-blind), deuteranopia (green-blind), and tritanopia (blue-blind) perceive colors. It recalculates the contrast ratio for each simulated pair so you can verify accessibility under different vision conditions.

faq.question: Can I use RGB or HSL values instead of HEX?
faq.answer: Currently the tool accepts HEX color codes (#rrggbb format). You can use the companion Color Converter & Picker tool to convert RGB or HSL values to HEX first, then paste the result here.

color-converter-picker.title: Color Converter & Picker

whatIs.heading: What Is a Color Converter?
whatIs.content: A color converter translates color values between different notation systems used in web development and design. The three most common formats are HEX (hexadecimal like #3b82f6), RGB (red-green-blue components like rgb(59, 130, 246)), and HSL (hue-saturation-lightness like hsl(217, 91%, 60%)). This tool lets you input a color in any supported format—or pick one visually—and instantly see the equivalent values in all formats, along with individual RGB channel values.

Enter colors in HEX (#3b82f6), RGB (rgb(59,130,246)), HSL (hsl(217,91%,60%)), or named CSS colors (blue, coral).
View the large color preview swatch below the input.

faq.question: What color formats are supported as input?
faq.answer: The tool accepts HEX values (#rgb, #rrggbb), RGB notation (rgb(r, g, b)), HSL notation (hsl(h, s%, l%)), and named CSS colors (red, cornflowerblue, etc.).

faq.question: Why does the color picker only show HEX output?
faq.answer: The native HTML color picker works with HEX values. Once a color is selected, the tool automatically converts it to all supported formats (HEX, RGB, HSL) displayed below.

faq.question: Does this support alpha/transparency?
faq.answer: Currently, the tool focuses on opaque colors. HEX with alpha (#rrggbbaa), RGBA, and HSLA input may be parsed but the alpha channel is not separately displayed.

faq.question: Is the conversion accurate?
faq.answer: Yes. The tool uses the culori library, a high-precision color science library that handles color space conversions accurately according to CSS Color Level 4 specifications.

color-space-extended.title: Color Space Explorer — OKLCH, OKLab, P3 & More
color-space-extended.description: Free online extended color space converter. Convert between HEX, RGB, HSL, HWB, OKLab, OKLCH, Linear sRGB, and Display P3 with CSS output. No sign-up required.

whatIs.heading: What Are Modern CSS Color Spaces?
whatIs.content: CSS Color Level 4 introduces several new color spaces beyond the traditional sRGB. OKLCH and OKLab provide perceptually uniform color manipulation, meaning equal numerical changes produce equal perceived color shifts. Display P3 enables wider-gamut colors on modern displays. HWB (Hue, Whiteness, Blackness) offers an intuitive alternative to HSL. This tool converts a single color input into all these representations with ready-to-use CSS syntax.

howToUse.heading: How to Use This Color Space Explorer
steps: Enter a HEX color using the color picker or text input.
View the color converted into 8 different color spaces.
Click Copy CSS next to any format to copy the CSS-ready value.
Use OKLCH values for perceptually uniform color palettes in modern CSS.

items.title: Modern CSS Design

items.title: Wide-Gamut Color Work

items.title: Color Science Education
items.description: Understand how the same color is represented across different color models.

faq.question: What is the advantage of OKLCH over HSL?
faq.answer: OKLCH is perceptually uniform, meaning equal changes in lightness, chroma, or hue produce visually equal changes. HSL is not perceptually uniform, so two colors with the same HSL saturation can appear very different in perceived brightness.

faq.question: Can browsers actually display Display P3 colors?
faq.answer: Yes, modern browsers on wide-gamut displays (most modern Macs, iPhones, and many newer monitors) can render Display P3 colors using CSS color() function.

faq.question: What is OKLab vs OKLCH?
faq.answer: OKLab uses L (lightness), a (green-red axis), b (blue-yellow axis) — a Cartesian model. OKLCH uses L (lightness), C (chroma/saturation), H (hue angle) — a cylindrical model. OKLCH is more intuitive for picking colors.

cookie-parser.title: Cookie Parser
cookie-parser.description: Free online cookie parser tool. Parse and inspect Set-Cookie headers and document.cookie strings to view names, values, and attributes.

whatIs.heading: What Is a Cookie Parser?
whatIs.content: HTTP cookies are small pieces of data that web servers send to browsers via the Set-Cookie response header. Browsers store these cookies and include them in subsequent requests using the Cookie header. Cookies are fundamental to web development — they manage user sessions, store preferences, track authentication state, and enable personalization. A Cookie Parser breaks down raw cookie strings into their individual components: the cookie name and value, along with attributes like Expires, Max-Age, Domain, Path, Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite. This tool supports parsing both Set-Cookie headers (server response format with attributes) and document.cookie strings (browser-side format with multiple key-value pairs).

howToUse.heading: How to Use the Cookie Parser
steps: Select the parsing mode: 'Parse Set-Cookie' for server response headers or 'Parse Cookie String' for browser-side cookie strings.
For Set-Cookie headers: paste the full header value (e.g., 'session_id=abc123; Path=/; HttpOnly; Secure'). The tool will extract the cookie name, value, and all attributes.
For Cookie strings: paste the document.cookie value (e.g., 'key1=value1; key2=value2'). The tool will split it into individual cookie key-value pairs.
Review the parsed output showing each component in a structured, easy-to-read format.

items.title: Debugging Authentication Issues
items.description: Inspect Set-Cookie headers from authentication endpoints to verify cookie attributes like HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite, and expiration are set correctly.

items.title: Security Auditing
items.description: Check whether cookies have proper security flags (Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite) to prevent XSS attacks, CSRF vulnerabilities, and cookie theft.

items.title: Cross-Domain Cookie Troubleshooting
items.description: Analyze Domain and Path attributes to diagnose issues with cookies not being sent to the expected subdomains or URL paths.

items.title: Session Management Review
items.description: Parse session cookies to verify expiration times, understand Max-Age settings, and ensure session cookies are configured according to security best practices.

faq.question: What is the difference between Set-Cookie and document.cookie?
faq.answer: Set-Cookie is an HTTP response header sent by the server to create or update a cookie, and includes attributes like Expires, Path, Domain, Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite. document.cookie is a JavaScript property in the browser that returns all non-HttpOnly cookies as a single string of 'key=value' pairs separated by semicolons, without the attributes.

faq.question: What does the HttpOnly flag mean?
faq.answer: The HttpOnly flag prevents JavaScript from accessing the cookie via document.cookie. This is a critical security measure that helps prevent cross-site scripting (XSS) attacks from stealing session cookies. Cookies with HttpOnly can only be sent with HTTP requests, not read by client-side scripts.

faq.question: What is the SameSite attribute?
faq.answer: SameSite controls whether cookies are sent with cross-site requests. 'Strict' means the cookie is only sent with same-site requests. 'Lax' (the default in most browsers) allows cookies on top-level navigations. 'None' allows cookies on all cross-site requests but requires the Secure flag. SameSite helps prevent CSRF attacks.

faq.question: How do Expires and Max-Age differ?
faq.answer: Expires sets an absolute date/time when the cookie expires (e.g., 'Fri, 31 Dec 2027 23:59:59 GMT'). Max-Age sets the cookie lifetime in seconds from the current time (e.g., '86400' for 24 hours). If both are set, Max-Age takes precedence. If neither is set, the cookie is a session cookie that is deleted when the browser closes.

cors-header-checker.title: CORS Header Checker
cors-header-checker.description: Free online CORS header checker and analyzer. Validate Access-Control headers, diagnose cross-origin issues, and verify preflight configuration.

whatIs.heading: What Is a CORS Header Checker?
whatIs.content: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) is a browser security mechanism that controls how web pages on one domain can request resources from a different domain. When a frontend application at https://myapp.com tries to fetch data from https://api.example.com, the browser checks the server's CORS headers to determine whether the request is allowed. A CORS Header Checker analyzes the response headers from an API server and evaluates whether they correctly permit cross-origin requests from your specified origin, HTTP method, and custom headers. It identifies common configuration mistakes such as missing Access-Control-Allow-Origin headers, wildcard origins combined with credentials, and disallowed methods or headers.

howToUse.heading: How to Use the CORS Header Checker
steps: Enter your frontend application's origin (e.g., 'https://myapp.com') in the Request Origin field.
Select the HTTP method your application uses to make the cross-origin request.
Paste the response headers from your API server into the Response Headers textarea. Enter one header per line in 'Key: Value' format.
Review the analysis results below. Green checkmarks indicate passing checks, yellow warnings suggest potential issues, and red crosses indicate blocking problems.

items.title: Debugging CORS Errors
items.description: When your browser console shows 'Access to fetch has been blocked by CORS policy', paste the response headers here to quickly identify what's missing or misconfigured.

items.title: Server Configuration Validation
items.description: Before deploying API changes, verify that your CORS headers correctly allow the expected origins, methods, and custom headers without being overly permissive.

items.title: Security Review
items.description: Audit your API's CORS configuration to ensure it follows security best practices — no wildcard origins with credentials, appropriate preflight caching, and minimal allowed headers.

items.title: Teaching & Learning CORS
items.description: Understand how different CORS header combinations affect browser behavior by experimenting with various configurations and seeing the analysis results in real-time.

faq.question: What is a CORS preflight request?
faq.answer: A preflight request is an automatic OPTIONS request sent by the browser before the actual request when using non-simple methods (PUT, PATCH, DELETE), custom headers, or certain content types. The server must respond with appropriate Access-Control-Allow-* headers. The Access-Control-Max-Age header controls how long the browser caches preflight results.

faq.question: Why can't I use Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * with credentials?
faq.answer: When a request includes credentials (cookies or HTTP authentication), the browser requires the server to respond with the exact origin in Access-Control-Allow-Origin, not the wildcard *. This prevents any website from making authenticated requests to your API. You must echo back the specific requesting origin.

faq.question: What are 'simple requests' that don't require preflight?
faq.answer: Simple requests use GET, HEAD, or POST methods with only standard headers (Accept, Accept-Language, Content-Language, Content-Type with values application/x-www-form-urlencoded, multipart/form-data, or text/plain). Any other combination triggers a preflight OPTIONS request.

faq.question: Does this tool send actual requests to test CORS?
faq.answer: No, this tool performs purely offline analysis. You paste the response headers and it evaluates them against your specified origin and method. To get the actual response headers, you can use browser DevTools Network tab, curl with -I flag, or any HTTP client.

whatIs.heading: What Is a Crontab Expression?
whatIs.content: A crontab (cron table) expression is a compact string of five fields that defines a recurring schedule on Unix-like systems. Each field represents minute, hour, day of month, month, and day of week. Cron expressions power scheduled tasks such as backups, report generation, log rotation, and automated deployments. This tool lets you either explain an existing cron expression in plain language or build one interactively by setting each field.

howToUse.heading: How to Use the Crontab Tool
steps: Select the 'Explain' tab to decode an existing cron expression.
Enter or paste a cron expression (e.g., '0 9 * * 1-5') to see its human-readable description.
Click any preset button to quickly load common schedules.
Switch to the 'Generate' tab to build a cron expression field by field.
Set the values for minute, hour, day of month, month, and day of week.
Copy the generated expression using the copy button.

items.title: Scheduled Backups

items.title: CI/CD Pipeline Triggers
items.description: Define scheduled triggers for continuous integration pipelines, nightly builds, or periodic test suites.

items.title: Log Rotation & Cleanup
items.description: Set up recurring jobs to compress, archive, or delete old log files to manage disk space.

items.title: Monitoring & Alerting
items.description: Schedule health checks, uptime monitors, or periodic report generation for system observability.

faq.question: What do the five fields in a cron expression mean?
faq.answer: From left to right: minute (0-59), hour (0-23), day of month (1-31), month (1-12), and day of week (0-6, where 0 is Sunday). An asterisk (*) means 'every' value for that field.

faq.question: What does */15 mean in a cron field?
faq.answer: The slash notation means 'every N units.' So */15 in the minute field means 'every 15 minutes,' which translates to minutes 0, 15, 30, and 45.

faq.question: Does this support 6-field or 7-field cron expressions?
faq.answer: This tool focuses on the standard 5-field Unix crontab format. Some systems (like Quartz or Spring) use 6 or 7 fields including seconds and year—those are not supported here.

faq.question: Can I use day names like MON-FRI?
faq.answer: The standard crontab format uses numbers (0-6 or 1-7) for days of the week. While some cron implementations accept three-letter abbreviations (MON, TUE, etc.), this tool expects numeric values or ranges like 1-5.

csp-header-builder.title: CSP Header Builder
csp-header-builder.description: Free online Content Security Policy (CSP) header builder and generator. Visually configure CSP directives, validate policies, and test URLs against your security rules.

whatIs.heading: What Is a CSP Header Builder?
whatIs.content: Content Security Policy (CSP) is a browser security standard that helps prevent cross-site scripting (XSS), clickjacking, and other code injection attacks by defining which sources of content are allowed to load on a web page. A CSP Header Builder provides a visual interface to construct valid CSP header strings without memorizing directive syntax. You toggle directives on or off, select allowed source values like 'self', 'unsafe-inline', or specific domains, and the tool generates the correct header string in real time. It also warns you about conflicting or insecure configurations, such as combining 'unsafe-inline' with 'strict-dynamic', or using 'unsafe-eval' which re-enables dangerous JavaScript execution.

howToUse.heading: How to Use the CSP Header Builder
steps: Enable the directives you need by toggling them on. Start with 'default-src' as a baseline fallback policy.
For each enabled directive, click the source values you want to allow (e.g., 'self', https:, data:). Selected values are highlighted in blue.
Add custom domains by typing them into the input field and pressing Enter or clicking Add (e.g., https://cdn.example.com).
Review any yellow warnings that appear — they indicate conflicting or insecure directive combinations.
Copy the generated CSP header string or HTML meta tag using the copy buttons.
Optionally, enter a test URL to check whether it would be allowed or blocked by your current policy.

items.title: Hardening Web Application Security
items.description: Build a strict CSP policy for your production website to block inline scripts, restrict resource origins, and prevent XSS attacks. Start with a restrictive default-src 'none' and selectively allow only the sources your application needs.

items.title: Migrating to CSP Incrementally
items.description: When adding CSP to an existing application, use the builder to experiment with different directive combinations, identify which sources your app requires, and gradually tighten the policy without breaking functionality.

items.title: Debugging CSP Violations
items.description: When your browser console shows CSP violation errors, use the test URL feature to check whether a specific resource URL is allowed by your current policy and adjust directives accordingly.

items.title: Generating Meta Tags for Static Sites
items.description: For static sites hosted on platforms where you cannot set HTTP headers, copy the generated HTML meta tag to embed the CSP policy directly in your HTML document's head section.

faq.question: What is the difference between CSP as an HTTP header and a meta tag?
faq.answer: Both methods deliver the same policy to the browser. The HTTP header (Content-Security-Policy) is set by the server and supports all directives. The HTML meta tag (meta http-equiv Content-Security-Policy) is embedded in the page and works for most directives, but does not support frame-ancestors, report-uri, or sandbox. The HTTP header is generally preferred because it is applied before the page HTML is parsed.

faq.question: What does 'strict-dynamic' do and why does it ignore 'unsafe-inline'?
faq.answer: 'strict-dynamic' tells the browser to trust scripts loaded by already-trusted scripts (via nonce or hash), even from new origins. When 'strict-dynamic' is present, the browser ignores 'unsafe-inline' and host-based allowlists for script-src. This is by design — it enables a nonce-based approach where only explicitly nonced scripts run, and they can dynamically load additional scripts.

faq.question: Should I always set default-src?
faq.answer: Yes, default-src acts as a fallback for any directive you don't explicitly set. If you define script-src but not style-src, the browser uses default-src for styles. Setting default-src to 'none' or 'self' establishes a secure baseline that you then relax per directive as needed.

faq.question: Why is 'unsafe-eval' dangerous?
faq.answer: 'unsafe-eval' allows the use of eval(), new Function(), setTimeout with strings, and other dynamic code execution APIs. These are common XSS attack vectors because they let an attacker execute arbitrary JavaScript if they can inject a string into your application. Avoid 'unsafe-eval' unless absolutely required by your framework or libraries.

faq.question: Does this tool validate the CSP header against the official specification?
faq.answer: This tool generates syntactically correct CSP headers and warns about common conflicting or insecure combinations. However, it does not perform exhaustive W3C specification validation. For production deployment, test your CSP in a staging environment using the browser's developer console to catch any violations.

whatIs.heading: What Is a CSS Unit Converter?
whatIs.content: A CSS unit converter translates values between different CSS measurement units such as pixels (px), root em (rem), em, points (pt), percentages (%), viewport width (vw), and viewport height (vh). Understanding how these units relate to each other is essential for building responsive web layouts that adapt to different screen sizes and user preferences.

howToUse.heading: How to Use the CSS Unit Converter

Select the source unit from the dropdown menu.

items.title: Responsive Design Calculations

items.title: Design System Implementation

items.title: Print Stylesheet Development
items.description: Convert between screen units (px, rem) and print units (pt) when creating CSS stylesheets for printed documents.

faq.question: What is the difference between rem and em?
faq.answer: rem (root em) is relative to the root element's (html) font size, making it consistent throughout the page. em is relative to the parent element's font size, which means it can compound when nested. For predictable sizing, rem is generally preferred.

faq.question: Why is 16px the default base font size?
faq.answer: 16px is the default font size set by most browsers for the root html element. When no custom font size is defined, 1rem equals 16px. This is the standard baseline used for rem and em calculations.

faq.question: When should I use viewport units (vw/vh)?
faq.answer: Viewport units are ideal for elements that should scale with the browser window, such as hero sections, full-screen backgrounds, or fluid typography. 1vw equals 1% of the viewport width, and 1vh equals 1% of the viewport height.

faq.question: How do percentage units work in CSS?
faq.answer: In this converter, percentage is calculated relative to the base font size (similar to em behavior for font-size). In actual CSS, percentages are relative to the parent element's corresponding property, which varies depending on the property being used.

csv-viewer-editor.title: CSV Viewer & Editor — Free Online
csv-viewer-editor.description: View, edit, sort, and export CSV data in a spreadsheet-like table. Supports custom delimiters (comma, tab, semicolon, pipe). Powered by PapaParse — no data leaves your browser.

whatIs.heading: What is a CSV Viewer & Editor?
whatIs.content: A CSV Viewer & Editor is an online tool that parses CSV (Comma-Separated Values) text into a visual table you can browse, sort, edit, and export. CSV is the most widely used format for exchanging tabular data between spreadsheets, databases, and programming languages. This tool uses PapaParse — the fastest in-browser CSV parser — to handle large files, custom delimiters, quoted fields, and edge cases. You can edit cell values directly in the table, sort columns numerically or alphabetically, and export the modified data back to CSV with a single click.

howToUse.heading: How to Use This Free Online CSV Viewer & Editor
steps: Paste your CSV data into the Input tab text area, or use the pre-loaded sample data.
Select the correct delimiter if your data uses tabs, semicolons, or pipes instead of commas.

faq.question: What delimiters are supported?
faq.answer: The tool supports four common delimiters: comma (,), tab (\t), semicolon (;), and pipe (|). Select the correct delimiter from the dropdown before parsing your data.

faq.question: Can I edit cell values?

faq.question: How do I export the edited data?
faq.answer: Click the 'Export to Input' button to regenerate the CSV string from the current table state and place it in the Input tab. You can also use the Copy button to copy the CSV directly to your clipboard.

faq.question: Does sorting modify my original data?
faq.answer: Sorting reorders the rows in the table view. When you export, the CSV will reflect the current sort order. Your original input text is not modified until you click 'Export to Input'.

faq.question: Is my data sent to a server?
faq.answer: No. All parsing, editing, and exporting happens entirely in your browser using PapaParse. No data is transmitted to any server.

faq.question: How large a CSV can this tool handle?
faq.answer: PapaParse is highly optimized and can handle files with tens of thousands of rows in the browser. However, rendering very large tables may slow down the page. For extremely large files, consider processing them with command-line tools.

data-format-converter.title: Data Format Converter — Free Online
data-format-converter.description: Convert between JSON, YAML, TOML, and CSV instantly with this free online data format converter. Swap formats with one click, no sign-up required.

whatIs.heading: What is a Data Format Converter?
whatIs.content: A data format converter is a tool that transforms structured data from one serialization format to another. Modern software development uses multiple data formats for different purposes: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is the dominant format for web APIs and configuration; YAML (YAML Ain't Markup Language) is popular for CI/CD pipelines, Kubernetes manifests, and human-editable configs; TOML (Tom's Obvious Minimal Language) is used by Rust's Cargo, Python's pyproject.toml, and other build tools; CSV (Comma-Separated Values) is the universal format for spreadsheets and tabular data. Converting between these formats manually is tedious and error-prone — this tool parses the input format, creates an intermediate data structure, and serializes it into the target format automatically.

howToUse.heading: How to Use This Free Online Data Format Converter
steps: Select the input format (JSON, YAML, TOML, or CSV) from the left dropdown.
Select the desired output format from the right dropdown.
Paste your data into the input text area. The conversion happens in real time.
Use the swap button (↔) to reverse input and output formats and switch the converted output back into the input field.
Click the Copy button to copy the converted result to your clipboard.

useCases.heading: Common Use Cases for Data Format Conversion

items.title: Converting API responses to YAML configs
items.description: Take JSON output from an API and convert it to YAML format for use in Kubernetes manifests, Docker Compose files, or Ansible playbooks.

items.title: Migrating configuration between tools
items.description: Convert TOML configuration (e.g. pyproject.toml) to JSON or YAML when switching build systems or tools that require a different format.

items.title: Exporting data to spreadsheets
items.description: Convert JSON arrays or YAML lists into CSV format that can be opened directly in Excel, Google Sheets, or other spreadsheet applications.

items.title: Importing CSV data into applications
items.description: Parse CSV files from databases or exports and convert them to JSON for use in web applications, APIs, or NoSQL databases.

items.title: Learning new data formats
items.description: Quickly see how the same data looks in different formats to understand YAML indentation, TOML table syntax, or CSV header conventions.

faq.question: Can all data be converted between all formats?
faq.answer: Not always. JSON, YAML, and TOML can represent nested objects and arrays, so they convert well between each other. CSV is inherently flat (rows and columns), so complex nested data may not convert cleanly to CSV. When converting to CSV, the tool flattens the top-level array of objects into columns and rows.

faq.question: Does YAML preserve data types during conversion?
faq.answer: YAML has richer type inference than JSON — for example, it can interpret 'true', 'yes', and 'on' as booleans, and bare numbers as integers or floats. This tool uses js-yaml's default schema which follows the YAML 1.2 spec, so type handling is standard and predictable.

faq.question: What TOML features are supported?
faq.answer: This tool uses smol-toml which supports TOML v1.0 including tables, inline tables, arrays of tables, multiline strings, datetime types, and all numeric formats. However, TOML does not support null values, so any null fields in your data will need to be handled.

faq.question: Is this tool free and private?
faq.answer: Yes. All conversions run entirely in your browser using JavaScript libraries (js-yaml, smol-toml, papaparse). No data is sent to any server, and no sign-up is required.

date-time-converter.title: Date-Time Converter with Timezone Support
date-time-converter.description: Free online date-time converter to convert dates and times across timezones. Supports ISO 8601, RFC 2822, Unix timestamps, and relative time — powered by native Intl APIs.

whatIs.heading: What Is a Date-Time Converter?
whatIs.content: A date-time converter translates a date and time from one timezone to another while presenting the result in multiple standard formats. This tool uses the browser's built-in Intl.DateTimeFormat API for accurate IANA timezone conversions — no external libraries required. Enter any date-time, choose a source and target timezone, and instantly see the converted result in ISO 8601, RFC 2822, Unix timestamps (seconds and milliseconds), locale format, and relative time.

howToUse.heading: How to Use the Date-Time Converter
steps: Enter a date-time string in the input field (e.g., '2024-06-15T14:30:00'), or click 'Now' to use the current time.
Select the source timezone that the input date-time is expressed in.
Select the target timezone you want to convert to.
View the converted time in six formats: ISO 8601, RFC 2822, Unix seconds, Unix milliseconds, locale format, and relative time.
Click the copy button next to any result row to copy it to your clipboard.

items.title: Scheduling Across Timezones
items.description: Convert meeting times between offices in different timezones to avoid confusion when planning international calls or deployments.

items.title: API & Log Timestamp Translation
items.description: Translate timestamps from server logs or API responses in UTC into your local timezone for faster debugging.

items.title: Cron Job Verification
items.description: Verify that scheduled cron jobs configured in UTC will trigger at the intended local time.

items.title: ISO 8601 & RFC 2822 Formatting
items.description: Generate correctly formatted date-time strings for HTTP headers, email Date fields, or API request bodies.

faq.question: Which timezones are supported?
faq.answer: The tool includes 13 of the most commonly used IANA timezones spanning UTC, the Americas, Europe, Asia, Australia, and the Pacific. It uses the browser's native Intl API, so conversions respect DST rules automatically.

faq.question: Does this tool handle Daylight Saving Time?
faq.answer: Yes. Because it relies on the browser's Intl.DateTimeFormat with IANA timezone identifiers, DST transitions are applied automatically based on the date you enter.

faq.question: What date-time formats can I enter?
faq.answer: Any format parseable by the JavaScript Date constructor works, including ISO 8601 (2024-06-15T14:30:00), date-only strings (2024-06-15), and common forms like 'Jun 15 2024 14:30'. The input is interpreted in the selected source timezone.

faq.question: Why do the Unix timestamps not change when I switch the target timezone?
faq.answer: Unix timestamps represent an absolute moment in time (seconds since the Unix epoch in UTC). They are identical regardless of which timezone the date is displayed in — only the human-readable formats change.

device-information.title: Device Information - Browser, Screen & Hardware Details
device-information.description: View detailed device and browser information including user agent, screen resolution, GPU, CPU cores, memory, connection type, and more — all detected in real time.

whatIs.heading: What Is the Device Information Viewer?
whatIs.content: The Device Information Viewer is a client-side tool that reads your browser and device properties using standard Web APIs such as the Navigator API, Screen API, WebGL, and the Network Information API. It displays a structured overview of your current browser identity, screen dimensions, system hardware, and GPU capabilities. All data is collected locally in your browser — nothing is sent to any server.

howToUse.heading: How to Use the Device Information Viewer
steps: Open the tool — your device information is automatically detected and displayed.
Browse through the four sections: Browser, Screen, System, and GPU.
Click the copy button next to any value to copy it to your clipboard.
Click the Refresh button to re-read all values (useful after resizing the window or changing orientation).
Use the displayed User Agent string and screen details for debugging responsive layouts or browser-specific issues.

items.title: Bug Reports & Support Tickets

items.title: Responsive Design Testing
items.description: Check your actual screen dimensions, pixel ratio, and orientation to verify how your website is being rendered on the current device.

items.title: Performance Diagnostics

items.title: User Agent Verification

faq.question: Is any data sent to a server?
faq.answer: No. All information is read using browser-native JavaScript APIs and stays entirely in your browser. Nothing is transmitted to any external server.

faq.question: Why do some values show N/A?
faq.answer: Some APIs like Device Memory and Network Information are not supported in all browsers (notably Safari and Firefox). When an API is unavailable, the tool displays N/A instead.

faq.question: How accurate is the GPU information?
faq.answer: GPU information is read via the WEBGL_debug_renderer_info WebGL extension. Most desktop browsers expose the real GPU name, but some privacy-focused browsers or configurations may mask or hide this data.

faq.question: Does the screen size change when I resize the browser window?
faq.answer: Screen width and height reflect the physical display dimensions, not the browser viewport. They remain constant regardless of window size. The pixel ratio may change if you move the window to a display with a different DPI.

diff-viewer.title: Text Diff Viewer & Comparator
diff-viewer.description: Free online diff viewer to compare two texts side by side or in unified format, highlighting additions, deletions, and changes instantly.

whatIs.heading: What Is a Diff Viewer?
whatIs.content: A diff viewer is a tool that compares two blocks of text and visually highlights the differences between them. It identifies lines that have been added, removed, or modified, making it easy to review changes at a glance. Diff tools are fundamental in software development for code reviews, merge conflict resolution, and tracking document revisions. This tool supports both side-by-side and unified diff formats, with the output generated entirely in your browser.

howToUse.heading: How to Use the Diff Viewer
steps: Paste the original text into the 'Original' textarea on the left.
Paste the modified text into the 'Modified' textarea on the right.
View the color-coded diff below: green for additions, red for deletions.
Use the 'Swap' button to reverse the comparison direction.
Switch between 'Side by Side' and 'Unified' tabs to change the diff format.
Copy the unified diff output using the copy button for use in patches or documentation.

items.title: Code Review
items.description: Compare original and modified source code to quickly identify what changed, making it easier to review pull requests or patches.

items.title: Configuration File Changes
items.description: Spot differences between two versions of config files (YAML, JSON, .env) to verify deployments or debug environment issues.

items.title: Document Revision Tracking
items.description: Compare drafts of documents, contracts, or articles to see exactly which sentences or paragraphs were edited.

items.title: Merge Conflict Resolution
items.description: Visualize conflicting changes from different branches to understand what each side modified before resolving the conflict.

faq.question: What diff formats are available?
faq.answer: The tool provides two views: Side by Side shows additions and deletions inline with color coding, and Unified generates a standard unified diff format commonly used in patches and version control systems.

faq.question: Can I compare binary files or images?
faq.answer: No, this tool is designed for plain text comparison. For binary files or images, use specialized diff tools in your IDE or version control system.

faq.question: Is my text sent to a server?
faq.answer: No. The comparison is performed entirely in your browser using the 'diff' JavaScript library. Your content never leaves your device.

faq.question: How does the unified diff output work?
faq.answer: The unified diff format shows context lines (prefixed with a space), additions (prefixed with +), and deletions (prefixed with -), along with line range headers. This format is compatible with tools like git apply and the patch command.

docker-run-to-compose.title: Docker Run to Compose Converter
docker-run-to-compose.description: Free online tool to convert docker run commands to docker-compose.yml format. Instantly transform container commands into composable YAML configurations.

whatIs.heading: What Is Docker Run to Compose Converter?
whatIs.content: Docker Run to Compose Converter is a utility that transforms long docker run CLI commands into clean, version-controlled docker-compose.yml service definitions. Instead of maintaining hard-to-read shell scripts with lengthy docker run commands, you can convert them into structured YAML files that are easier to manage, share, and deploy across teams.

howToUse.heading: How to Use Docker Run to Compose Converter
steps: Paste your full docker run command into the input field, including all flags and options.
The tool automatically parses the command and generates the equivalent docker-compose.yml output.
Review the generated YAML to verify ports, volumes, environment variables, and other settings.
Click the copy button to copy the docker-compose.yml content to your clipboard.
Save the output to a docker-compose.yml file in your project directory.

items.title: Migrating to Docker Compose
items.description: Quickly convert existing docker run commands from documentation or scripts into docker-compose.yml files for better reproducibility and team collaboration.

items.title: Documentation & Onboarding
items.description: Transform complex docker run commands into readable compose files that new team members can understand and modify easily.

items.title: CI/CD Pipeline Setup
items.description: Generate compose files from ad-hoc docker run commands used during development to create standardized deployment configurations.

items.title: Multi-container Applications
items.description: Convert individual docker run commands for each service into a unified docker-compose.yml with all services defined together.

faq.question: Does this tool support all docker run flags?
faq.answer: The tool supports the most commonly used flags including -p (ports), -v (volumes), -e (environment variables), --name, --restart, --network, --hostname, and --workdir. Some advanced flags may not be converted automatically.

faq.question: Which docker-compose version does the output use?
faq.answer: The output uses the modern Compose Specification format (without a version key), which is compatible with Docker Compose V2 and later.

faq.question: Can I convert multiple docker run commands at once?
faq.answer: Currently the tool converts one command at a time. For multi-service setups, convert each docker run command separately and combine the service definitions into a single docker-compose.yml file.

faq.question: Is the conversion done locally or sent to a server?
faq.answer: All conversion is performed entirely in your browser using JavaScript. No data is sent to any server, ensuring your commands and configurations remain private.

dockerfile-linter.title: Dockerfile Linter
dockerfile-linter.description: Analyze your Dockerfile for best practices, security issues, and optimization opportunities with instant feedback and actionable suggestions.

whatIs.heading: What Is a Dockerfile Linter?
whatIs.content: A Dockerfile Linter is a static analysis tool that examines your Dockerfile instructions and flags potential issues. It checks for common mistakes, security vulnerabilities (like running as root), deprecated syntax, inefficient layering, missing health checks, and unpinned base image tags. Similar to tools like Hadolint, it provides severity-rated feedback with concrete suggestions to help you build smaller, safer, and more maintainable container images.

howToUse.heading: How to Use the Dockerfile Linter
steps: Paste your Dockerfile content into the text area.
The linter automatically analyzes every instruction and displays a categorized list of findings.
Each result includes a line number, severity level (error, warning, or info), a rule ID, a description, and a fix suggestion.
Review the summary counts at the top, then address errors first, followed by warnings and informational hints.

items.title: Pre-Push Auditing
items.description: Audit Dockerfiles before pushing to a container registry to catch issues early.

items.title: CI/CD Best Practices
items.description: Enforce Dockerfile best practices in CI/CD pipelines to maintain consistent image quality.

items.title: Learning Docker
items.description: Learn Docker best practices with real-time feedback and actionable suggestions on your own Dockerfiles.

items.title: Code Review
items.description: Review Dockerfiles during code reviews to catch security issues, optimization opportunities, and deprecated syntax.

faq.question: What rules does the Dockerfile linter check?
faq.answer: The linter checks for over 15 rules across three severity levels: errors (missing FROM, latest tag, ADD vs COPY, duplicate CMD), warnings (apt-get flags, sudo usage, deprecated MAINTAINER, missing HEALTHCHECK/USER, shell-form CMD), and info (combining RUN layers, .dockerignore, absolute WORKDIR paths).

faq.question: Is this linter equivalent to Hadolint?
faq.answer: This tool covers the most common and impactful Hadolint rules in a browser-based interface with no installation required. For comprehensive linting in CI/CD, consider using Hadolint alongside this tool for quick checks.

faq.question: Why should I pin my base image version?
faq.answer: Using 'latest' or an untagged base image means your builds may break unexpectedly when the upstream image is updated. Pinning a specific version (e.g., node:20-alpine) ensures reproducible, predictable builds.

faq.question: Why is running as root in a container a security risk?
faq.answer: Running as root inside a container increases the attack surface. If an attacker compromises the application, they have root privileges within the container, which can make container escapes more dangerous. Adding a USER instruction mitigates this risk.

ed25519-ssh-key-generator.title: Ed25519 Key Generator — Sign & Verify
ed25519-ssh-key-generator.description: Free online Ed25519 key pair generator with message signing and signature verification. Uses the Web Crypto API. No sign-up, runs in your browser.

whatIs.heading: What Is Ed25519?
whatIs.content: Ed25519 is a modern elliptic-curve digital signature algorithm that provides high security with short keys and fast performance. It is based on the Edwards-curve Digital Signature Algorithm (EdDSA) using Curve25519. Ed25519 is used in SSH, TLS, cryptocurrency wallets, and many authentication protocols. A 32-byte public key and 64-byte signature provide 128-bit security.

howToUse.heading: How to Use This Ed25519 Key Generator
steps: Click Generate Ed25519 Key Pair to create a new public/private key pair.
Copy the public key (SPKI PEM) or the raw Base64 format for use in your application.
Keep the private key (PKCS8 PEM) secure — never share it.
Use the Sign section to sign messages with the generated private key.
Use the Verify section to check a signature against the public key.

items.title: SSH Key Generation
items.description: Generate Ed25519 keys for SSH authentication, the recommended algorithm for modern OpenSSH.

items.title: Digital Signature Testing
items.description: Test signing and verification workflows for APIs, JWTs, or blockchain applications.

items.title: Learning Cryptography
items.description: Understand how public-key cryptography works by experimenting with key generation, signing, and verification.

faq.question: Is Ed25519 better than RSA?
faq.answer: Ed25519 offers equivalent or better security with much smaller key sizes (32 bytes vs 256+ bytes for RSA-2048) and significantly faster operations. It is the recommended algorithm for new SSH keys.

faq.question: Which browsers support Ed25519 in Web Crypto?
faq.answer: As of 2025 Chrome, Edge, Firefox, and Safari all support Ed25519 in the Web Crypto API. Older browsers may not.

faq.question: Is my private key sent anywhere?
faq.answer: No. Key generation uses your browser Web Crypto API. No data leaves your device.

email-normalizer.title: Email Normalizer
email-normalizer.description: Normalize and deduplicate email addresses by removing dots, plus aliases, and provider-specific suffixes to find canonical forms.
email-normalizer.whatIs: The Email Normalizer tool standardizes email addresses by applying provider-specific normalization rules. It removes dots and plus-aliases from Gmail addresses, plus-aliases from Outlook/Hotmail, dash-suffixes from Yahoo, and lowercases all addresses. This helps identify duplicate accounts and clean up mailing lists by converting addresses to their canonical form.
email-normalizer.howToUse: Paste one or more email addresses (one per line) into the input area. The tool automatically normalizes each address based on the detected provider. Use the toggle options to control whether plus-aliases and dots are removed. Results are displayed in a table showing the original address, normalized version, detected provider, and changes applied. Duplicate normalized addresses are highlighted. Click 'Copy normalized emails' to copy the deduplicated results.
useCases: Clean up mailing lists by removing duplicate email addresses created with aliases
Detect users who registered multiple accounts using Gmail dot tricks or plus-addressing
Standardize email databases before migration or merge operations
Validate and canonicalize email addresses during data import processes
Audit sign-up lists for duplicate or alias-based registrations

faq.question: Why are dots removed only for Gmail addresses?
faq.answer: Gmail ignores dots in the local part of an email address, so john.doe(at)gmail.com and johndoe(at)gmail.com deliver to the same mailbox. Other providers like Outlook treat dots as significant characters, so removing them would change the recipient.

faq.question: What is plus-addressing and which providers support it?
faq.answer: Plus-addressing (also called sub-addressing) lets you add a +tag after your username (e.g., user+newsletter(at)gmail.com). Gmail, Outlook, and many other providers support this. Emails sent to the tagged address are delivered to the base address. This tool strips the plus-tag to find the canonical address.

faq.question: How does Yahoo alias detection work?
faq.answer: Yahoo uses a dash-based system for disposable addresses. A Yahoo user can create aliases like username-keyword(at)yahoo.com. The tool strips everything after the first dash to find the base Yahoo address.

faq.question: Does this tool validate whether an email address actually exists?
faq.answer: No, this tool only normalizes the format of email addresses based on known provider rules. It does not verify whether the mailbox exists or is active. Use a dedicated email verification service for deliverability checks.

emoji-picker.title: Emoji Picker & Search
emoji-picker.description: Free online emoji picker and search tool. Browse, search, and copy emojis by category — smileys, animals, food, symbols, flags, and more. Click any emoji to copy it to your clipboard instantly.

whatIs.content: The Emoji Picker & Search is a fast, browser-based tool for finding and copying emojis. It organizes hundreds of popular emojis into intuitive categories — Smileys & People, Animals & Nature, Food & Drink, Activities, Travel & Places, Objects, Symbols, and Flags. Use the search bar to filter by name, click any emoji to copy it to your clipboard, and access your recently used emojis for quick reuse.

howToUse.heading: How to Use the Emoji Picker

Click a category tab to narrow the grid to a specific emoji group.
Hover over any emoji to see a large preview and its name.

Check the 'Recent' tab to quickly access emojis you have used before.

faq.question: How many emojis are included?

faq.question: Are my recently used emojis saved?
faq.answer: Yes. Your last 20 used emojis are stored in your browser's localStorage and persist across sessions. They appear under the 'Recent' category tab for quick access.

faq.question: Do emojis render the same on all devices?
faq.answer: Emojis are Unicode characters, so they are supported everywhere. However, their visual appearance varies by operating system and browser — Apple, Google, Microsoft, and Samsung each have their own emoji designs.

encryption-decryption.title: AES Encryption & Decryption Tool
encryption-decryption.description: Encrypt and decrypt text with AES-GCM or AES-CBC using this free online tool. Password-based key derivation via PBKDF2. No sign-up required, runs entirely in your browser.

whatIs.heading: What Is AES Encryption?
whatIs.content: AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) is a symmetric-key encryption algorithm adopted by the U.S. government and used worldwide to protect sensitive data. It operates on fixed 128-bit blocks and supports key sizes of 128, 192, or 256 bits. AES-GCM (Galois/Counter Mode) provides both encryption and authentication in a single operation, detecting any tampering with the ciphertext. AES-CBC (Cipher Block Chaining) is a classic mode that chains blocks together but does not include built-in authentication. This tool derives a 256-bit AES key from your password using PBKDF2 with 100,000 iterations and a random salt, ensuring strong key derivation even from weak passwords.

howToUse.heading: How to Use This AES Encryption Tool
steps: Select the Encrypt or Decrypt tab.
Choose the algorithm (AES-GCM is recommended for authenticated encryption).
Select the output format — Base64 for compact output or Hex for a hexadecimal representation.
Enter your plaintext (or ciphertext for decryption) and a password.
Click the button — the result appears below with a copy option.

useCases.heading: Common Use Cases for AES Encryption

items.title: Encrypting sensitive notes or messages
items.description: Protect private messages, API keys, or credentials before sharing them through insecure channels like email or chat.

items.title: Client-side encryption before cloud upload
items.description: Encrypt files or data in the browser before uploading to cloud storage, ensuring the provider cannot read your content.

items.title: Testing encryption workflows
items.description: Developers can quickly test AES encryption and decryption logic, verify output formats, and debug interoperability issues.

items.title: Secure clipboard sharing
items.description: Encrypt text, share the ciphertext with a colleague, and provide the password through a separate channel for secure communication.

faq.question: What is the difference between AES-GCM and AES-CBC?
faq.answer: AES-GCM provides both encryption and authentication (AEAD) — it detects if the ciphertext has been tampered with. AES-CBC only encrypts and does not verify integrity. For most use cases, AES-GCM is the recommended choice.

faq.question: How is the encryption key derived from my password?
faq.answer: The tool uses PBKDF2 with SHA-256, 100,000 iterations, and a random 16-byte salt to derive a 256-bit AES key from your password. The salt is prepended to the ciphertext so decryption can regenerate the same key.

faq.question: Can I decrypt data encrypted by another AES tool?
faq.answer: Only if the other tool uses the same format: PBKDF2 key derivation with matching parameters, and salt + IV prepended to the ciphertext. This tool uses its own format, so it is designed to decrypt its own output.

faq.question: Is my password or data sent to a server?
faq.answer: No. All encryption and decryption happens locally in your browser using the Web Crypto API. No data or passwords ever leave your device.

env-yaml-json-converter.title: .env / YAML / JSON Converter — Free Online
env-yaml-json-converter.description: Convert between .env, JSON, and YAML formats instantly with this free online converter. Transform environment variable files, configuration formats, and dotenv files with one click.

whatIs.heading: What is an .env / YAML / JSON Converter?
whatIs.content: An .env / YAML / JSON converter is a tool that transforms configuration data between three of the most common formats used in software development. The .env format (dotenv) stores environment variables as simple KEY=VALUE pairs and is the standard for managing secrets and configuration in Node.js, Python, Ruby, and many other ecosystems. JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is the universal data interchange format used by APIs, package managers, and configuration files. YAML (YAML Ain't Markup Language) is favored for its human readability and is widely used in Docker Compose, Kubernetes, GitHub Actions, and CI/CD pipelines. Converting between these formats manually is tedious and error-prone — this tool parses the input format and serializes it into the target format automatically, preserving all key-value pairs.

howToUse.heading: How to Use This Free Online .env / YAML / JSON Converter
steps: Select the input format (.env, JSON, or YAML) from the left dropdown.
Select the desired output format from the right dropdown.
Paste your configuration data into the input text area. The conversion happens in real time.
Review the converted output in the output area below.
Click the Copy button to copy the converted result to your clipboard.

useCases.heading: Common Use Cases for .env / YAML / JSON Conversion

items.title: Converting .env files to JSON for application config
items.description: Transform dotenv environment variable files into JSON objects for use in application configuration, settings files, or API payloads.

items.title: Generating .env files from YAML configs
items.description: Extract key-value pairs from YAML configuration files (like Docker Compose environment sections) and output them as .env files for local development.

items.title: Converting JSON config to YAML for Kubernetes
items.description: Take JSON configuration objects and convert them to YAML format for use in Kubernetes ConfigMaps, Helm values files, or other YAML-based orchestration tools.

items.title: Migrating environment variables between platforms
items.description: Convert environment variables between formats when migrating between deployment platforms like Heroku (.env), AWS (JSON), or Kubernetes (YAML).

items.title: Sharing configuration with team members
items.description: Convert configuration between formats depending on what tools and workflows your team uses — .env for local development, YAML for CI/CD, JSON for documentation.

faq.question: Does this tool support nested objects?
faq.answer: This converter works with flat key-value pairs, which is the common denominator across all three formats. The .env format only supports flat key=value pairs, so nested JSON or YAML structures are not supported. For nested data conversion, use the Data Format Converter tool.

faq.question: How are comments handled in .env files?
faq.answer: Lines starting with # in .env files are treated as comments and are ignored during parsing. Comments are not preserved in the output since JSON and YAML key-value mappings do not have a standard way to represent inline .env comments.

faq.question: Are quoted values in .env files handled correctly?
faq.answer: Yes. Values wrapped in single quotes or double quotes in .env files are automatically unquoted during parsing. For example, DB_PASSWORD="my secret" will be parsed as the value my secret without the surrounding quotes.

faq.question: Is this tool free and private?
faq.answer: Yes. All conversions run entirely in your browser using JavaScript. No data is sent to any server, and no sign-up is required. Your configuration data and secrets never leave your machine.

eta-calculator.title: ETA Calculator — Estimated Time of Completion
eta-calculator.description: Free online ETA calculator. Enter total items, completed count, and elapsed time to estimate remaining time and completion time. No sign-up required.

whatIs.heading: What Is an ETA Calculator?
whatIs.content: An ETA (Estimated Time of Arrival/Completion) calculator predicts when a task will finish based on current progress and elapsed time. It calculates the processing rate and extrapolates to estimate remaining time. This is useful for batch processing, data migrations, build pipelines, and any long-running operation.
